title: Devil's food cake
subtitle: American dark chocolate layer cake
excerpt: A deeply dark, moist American chocolate cake with a tight, tender crumb. Hot coffee blooms the cocoa and deepens the chocolate flavour. Two layers, filled and covered with dark chocolate frosting.
sourceNotes: Devil's food cake is an American confection documented from the early twentieth century, distinct from chocolate cake by its use of bicarbonate of soda with acidic ingredients (buttermilk, coffee) which darken the crumb and produce a tighter, moister texture. The formula (cocoa bloomed in hot liquid, buttermilk, and bicarbonate) is consistent across American domestic cookery.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unfrosted layers. Frost after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 4 days at room temperature. Layers can be baked 2 days ahead and stored wrapped.

glossaryTerms.definition: Dissolving cocoa powder in a hot liquid — coffee, boiling water, or hot milk — before adding it to a cake batter. Heat opens up the cocoa particles and releases more flavour compounds, producing a significantly deeper chocolate colour and intensity than adding cocoa directly to the dry ingredients.

content.text: The characteristic darkness of this cake comes from two things:
in hot coffee, and the reaction of bicarbonate of soda with acidic buttermilk. Both darken the crumb. The coffee does not make the cake taste of coffee; it makes it taste more intensely of chocolate.
Whisk the {{cocoa-powder}} into the hot {{black-coffee}} until smooth. Leave to cool to room temperature.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease two 20 cm round tins and line the bases. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} (for the cake) and {{light-brown-sugar}} together until pale and fluffy. Beat in the {{free-range-eggs}} and {{vanilla-extract}}. Whisk the {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}} together. Fold the dry ingredients into the batter in three additions, alternating with the {{buttermilk}}. Stir in the cooled cocoa mixture until smooth.
Divide the batter evenly between the tins. Bake for 30–35 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack to cool completely.

Melt the {{dark-chocolate}} in a bowl over barely simmering water. Leave to cool to room temperature. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} (for the frosting) until very pale and fluffy, about 4 minutes. Beat in the {{icing-sugar}} in two additions, then beat in the cooled chocolate and {{double-cream}} until smooth. Sandwich the cooled layers with a third of the frosting; spread the rest over the top and sides.
